title: Chapter 21: The Server
content: The maintenance log blinked.

Jane had trained herself not to react to the ping — it was just another system notification in a château full of them, the digital hum of a building that never truly slept. She kept her eyes on the inventory spreadsheet, her fingers moving across the keyboard at a pace that suggested nothing more than afternoon diligence.
But the subject line made her stomach drop.
**MAINT-0047: Main valve found. Pressure critical. 72-hour replacement window.**
She clicked. Read. Read again.
Bertha had done it.

Six years ago, when Bertha Mason was still Mrs. Rochester — still the woman who signed the checks and walked the halls with authority instead of exile — she had built a back door into Thornfield's financial server. Not for sabotage. For survival. A woman married to a man like Edward Rochester learned to keep exits open, even digital ones. The back door had sat dormant all this time, a ghost in the system, waiting.
Now Bertha had used it. And what she'd found behind the main valve was staggering.

Jane scrolled through the attached data summary, her breath shallow. Full supply chain records stretching back a decade. Vendor payments that led to shell companies in Cyprus, Malta, the Isle of Man. Money laundering trails so intricate they might have been lace — delicate, beautiful, and entirely corrupt. The textile fraud was there, yes, but it was the scaffolding for something larger: a financial architecture designed to move dirty money through the legitimate fashion industry like dye through silk. Thornfield wasn't just complicit. Thornfield was the loom.
Her hands wanted to shake. She didn't let them.

Instead, she read the auto-delete protocol at the bottom of the file. The server had been designed with a failsafe: if data wasn't transferred to external storage within seventy-two hours of unauthorized access, the files would self-destruct. Overwritten sector by sector. Irrecoverable.
Seventy-two hours.
She glanced at the timestamp. The clock had started eleven minutes ago.

Jane closed the maintenance log and opened her calendar. Three days until the auto-delete. She needed a portable drive — something large enough to hold terabytes of financial records and encrypted well enough to survive transport. She needed to get that drive past Edward's security scanners, load the data, and get it out again. Every exit in the château was logged. Every item that passed through the front checkpoint was scanned and catalogued. A hard drive would be flagged instantly.

She sat in the archive for thirty more minutes, updating spreadsheets, answering a calendar notification about the Paris Fashion Week seating chart, and letting the problem turn itself over in the back of her mind like a stone in a river.
Then the answer came. It was so simple she almost laughed.

Staff. The staff went in and out with minimal screening — personal bags, shopping runs, supply trips. Edward trusted his security perimeter because he trusted the hierarchy he'd built inside it. The people at the bottom were invisible to him. They always had been.

She found Adèle in the workroom, arranging muslin swatches by color gradient. The girl's hands were quick and precise, her concentration absolute. She looked up when Jane entered, and something in her expression shifted — a subtle alertness, like a bird that had learned to watch the sky.
Jane closed the door. Not locked — locked doors drew attention.
"I need your help," she said.
Adèle set down the swatch she was holding. A strip of pale celadon against her fingers. "You've never asked before."

"I've never needed to." Jane crossed to the window, positioning herself where she could see the corridor. "I need a portable drive. A specific model — a Samsung T7 Shield, two terabytes. It's small. About the size of a credit card, maybe twice as thick."
"A drive." Adèle's voice was flat. Not confused — processing. "For the computers here."

"For data that exists here. Data that won't exist in three days if I don't move it." Jane turned from the window. "The château scanners will flag electronics. But you're going to Paris tomorrow for supplies. You can buy it there. Bring it back."

Adèle's hands had stopped moving. They rested on the muslin, and Jane could see the fine tremor in her fingers. The girl was nineteen — old enough to understand that this wasn't an errand, young enough that the understanding sat on her like a weight too large for her frame.
"What's on the drive?" Adèle asked.
"The truth. About Thornfield. About everything." Jane held her gaze. "I can't tell you more than that. Not because I don't trust you — because the less you know, the safer you are."
"What happens if we're caught?"
The honesty of the question demanded honesty in return. "I don't know."
Adèle swallowed. The tremor in her hands didn't stop, but she didn't pull them back from the muslin either. She was holding her ground.
"But I know what happens if we don't try," Jane said. "Nothing. All of this continues. The fraud, the corruption, the people who've been hurt — it all keeps going. And we'll have done nothing."

The workroom was quiet except for the distant clatter of dishes from the kitchen below. Somewhere in the château, Edward's security system hummed its quiet surveillance. The clock was already at seventy-one hours.

"I'll need it small enough to hide in a book," Jane said. "I'll tell you the title. You can buy both at the same shop — there's a librarian on Rue de Rivoli who does custom book hollowing. I'll text you the address."
Adèle looked down at her hands. At the celadon muslin, the steady gradient of color she'd been building. Then she looked up.
"Tomorrow. I leave at seven."

Jane nodded. "Bring it back inside a shopping bag with the fabric swatches. The scanners at the staff entrance are set for weapons and electronics larger than a tablet. Something that small, surrounded by textiles — it should read as part of the packaging."
"Should."
"Will," Jane corrected. She didn't have room for 'should.'
Adèle exhaled. Then she picked up the celadon swatch and returned it to its place in the gradient, as if the conversation were just another element to be arranged and set in order. "I'll do it."

The next morning, Jane descended to breakfast with the same measured steps she'd used every morning for months. Her engagement ring caught the light from the corridor windows — a band of cold fire on her left hand. She wore a charcoal dress, simple and severe, her hair pinned back. The picture of composure.

Edward was already at the table. The breakfast room faced east, and morning light spilled across the white tablecloth like something in a painting. Coffee steamed in a porcelain cup. Croissants sat in a linen-lined basket. The scene was perfect. The scene was a performance, and they were both performing.
"Good morning." He looked up from his phone. His eyes moved over her the way they always did — thorough, appreciative, proprietary. "You look tired."
"I was up late with the Paris schedule. The seating changes alone took three hours."
"You work too hard." He said it with a smile that meant: I like that you work too hard. It kept her busy. It kept her in the archive, out of the rooms where decisions were made.
She poured her coffee. "Someone has to."

They ate. They talked about the week ahead — fittings, fabric deliveries, a dinner with Blanche Ingram's family on Thursday. Jane contributed the right observations at the right moments. She laughed at his jokes. She reached across the table to touch his wrist when he made a dry comment about a rival house, and felt him still slightly under her fingers, pleased.
Sixty-four hours.

She went to the archive. She catalogued textile samples from the 1970s. She answered emails. She reviewed the guest list for the Paris showcase and made notes on seating proximity — who should be placed near whom, who should be kept at arm's length. The precision of the work anchored her, even as the number in her head counted down.
At lunch, Edward mentioned Bertha.

He did it casually, the way a person might reference bad weather from a season ago — a flicker of inconvenience, already past. "That unfortunate situation before your time," he said, passing the bread basket. "A woman who couldn't handle the pressure of this world. I don't speak of it often."
Jane took the basket. "I understand."

Do you? his eyes asked. She could feel him watching her across the table — the slight tilt of his head, the way his fingers stilled on the edge of his plate. He wasn't sharing a confidence. He was dropping a stone into still water and counting the ripples.

"She had a breakdown, I'm told," he continued. "Before she left. Delusions. Paranoia. She became convinced that the house was — " He waved his hand. "Recording her. Watching her. As if Thornfield were some kind of prison."
Jane's smile didn't waver. Her pulse spiked — a hard, fast drumbeat in her throat — but her face was a mask she'd spent months perfecting. "That must have been difficult for you."
"It was." He met her eyes. "I'm grateful to have found someone steadier."
She reached for her wine. The glass was smooth and cold against her fingers. "You have."
He smiled. The conversation moved on.

But Jane felt the shape of the test beneath his words. He suspected something — not the specifics, perhaps, but a wrongness in the arrangement. Edward Rochester was a man who trusted his instincts the way other men trusted ledgers, and his instincts were telling him that something was off. The mention of Bertha wasn't nostalgia. It was a probe.

She kept her composure through the afternoon. Through the planning session where they reviewed mood boards for the Paris collection. Through dinner, where Grace Poole served roast duck and Edward told a story about a buyer in Milan that made even Grace's stone face crack a smile. Through the hour afterward, when they sat in the salon and he read aloud from a review of the upcoming collections, his voice warm and unhurried, while Jane leaned against his shoulder and counted the hours in her head like a prisoner counting bars.
Fifty-three hours when she finally went to bed.

She lay in the dark and listened to the château settle around her — the creak of old beams, the distant hum of the server room three floors below, the soft tick of the radiator cooling. Somewhere in Paris, Adèle was sleeping in her apartment with a shopping list that included silk habotai, wool crepe, and two terabytes of encrypted storage.
Sleep came slowly, and when it did, it was thin and restless.
Adèle returned at dusk the following day.

Jane was in the archive when she heard the staff door — the distant beep of the scanner, the murmur of voices. She forced herself to wait ten minutes. To finish the entry she was typing. To close the application, shut down the terminal, and walk to the workroom at a pace that suggested mild curiosity rather than desperation.

Adèle was unpacking shopping bags when Jane entered. The room smelled of fabric — the warm wool scent of crepe, the cool chemical crispness of new synthetics, the faintly dusty sweetness of raw silk. Swatches were spread across the table in careful piles. It looked exactly like what it was supposed to look like: a supply run for the atelier.
"I brought the book you asked about," Adèle said. Her voice was steady. Her hands were not.
She reached into the last bag and pulled out a volume — *The House of Schiaparelli: Shocking Fashion*, a heavy art-book format with a matte black cover. She set it on the table between them.
Jane opened it.

The first fifty pages were intact — glossy photographs of Elsa Schiaparelli's surrealist collaborations, the lobsters and the tears and the skeleton dress. Then the pages changed. Someone had cut a precise rectangular cavity into the remaining pages, smooth edges, professional work. Nestled inside, matte black and no larger than a playing card, was a Samsung T7 Shield.

Jane picked it up. The metal was cold against her palm — dense, solid, impossibly small for what it contained. Two terabytes. Enough to hold an empire's worth of secrets. She turned it over, feeling the weight of it, the slight texture of the rubberized casing against her fingertips. Then she placed it back in the cavity and closed the book.
The cover clicked shut — a soft, satisfying sound, like a door locking into place.
"Perfect choice," she said. Her voice came out even, conversational. "The Schiaparelli chapter will be very useful."
Adèle's tiny smile was the bravest thing Jane had ever seen.
"How was the rest of your trip?" Jane asked, sliding the book onto the reference shelf as naturally as she would have shelved any other acquisition.
"Efficient." Adèle folded the empty shopping bag. "The habotai supplier had the ecru I wanted. And I found a new source for metallic thread — they'll send samples next week."
"Good." Jane was already scanning the corridor. Empty. "Thank you, Adèle."
The girl nodded once, firmly, and turned back to her fabric. The conversation was over. It had never happened.
Forty-eight hours remained.

Jane lay in bed that night, the book on the shelf three rooms away, the drive inside it humming with potential energy like a bomb. She'd verified the specs: the T7 Shield transferred data at up to 1,050 MB/s over USB-C. Two terabytes at that speed should take just over thirty minutes, but accounting for the archive terminal's older hardware and the encryption overhead, she was budgeting forty. Forty minutes of exposure. Forty minutes of sitting at the archive terminal with the drive connected and the server open, watching a progress bar and listening for footsteps.

The archive was in the east wing, two corridors from the master suite. Edward was a light sleeper when something was on his mind, and something was definitely on his mind. The mention of Bertha at lunch had made that clear. If he woke and found her side of the bed empty, he would come looking.

She needed a window. She'd studied the security rotation for weeks — Grace Poole's patrol passed the archive at 1:15 AM and again at 3:30 AM. The gap between them was her best shot. If she started at 1:45, she'd be done by 2:25. Back in bed by 2:30, the sheets still warm on her side, Edward none the wiser.

She set her phone alarm for 1:45 AM. Vibrating only. She placed the phone face-down on the nightstand and lay in the dark, listening to Edward breathe beside her. His breathing was slow and even tonight — the sleep of a man who believed his house was secure.

Her mind wouldn't quiet. She ran the sequence again. The walk to the archive. The key code — she'd memorized it weeks ago, during a casual conversation with Grace about building access protocols. The drive, slipped from its paper cavity. The terminal login, Bertha's back door credentials. The transfer. The wait.
Forty minutes.

Anything could happen in forty minutes. A noise could wake Edward. Grace could change her routine. A server alert could ping the security desk. The building itself — old, temperamental, full of noises that shouldn't exist — could conspire against her with a dropped pipe or a blown fuse at exactly the wrong moment.
She stared at the ceiling. The plasterwork was Baroque, ornate with vines and flowers and faces that seemed to shift in the dark, their expressions ambiguous — pitying or mocking, she couldn't tell.
Forty-eight hours. Forty minutes of exposure. One wrong move and everything fell apart.

The clock was ticking. She could almost hear it — not the phone's silent alarm, not the old radiator's tick, but something deeper. A countdown buried in the architecture of the house itself, in the server room below, in the data that was even now beginning to degrade, sector by sector, toward oblivion.
Jane closed her eyes.
At 1:45, she would rise. At 1:50, she would plug in the drive. At 2:30, the data would be safe — or she would be caught. There was no third option.
The minutes slid past like water through a cracked valve.
She didn't sleep.
